summary: The reproduction builds CoreDNS with DNSSEC signing enabled, starts the server, and sends a stream of unique random subdomain queries with DO=1 to trigger per-query cryptographic signing. The PoC measures the latency difference between unique-name queries (forcing full ECDSA signing) and cached queries (no signing) to demonstrate the CPU exhaustion vector.
expected_security_impact: Unique random subdomain queries with DO=1 should be significantly slower than cached repeated queries because each unique name forces a full cryptographic signing operation (ECDSA P-256). The latency ratio should exceed 5x, confirming that per-query signing is CPU-bound and can be exploited for denial of service by sending many unique names.
actual_observed_result: PoC was not executed in this session. The reproduction bundle was generated for independent execution via ./reproduction/run.sh.
verification_method: The verify.sh script inside the container builds CoreDNS, generates DNSSEC keys, starts the server, then runs exploit.sh which sends 200 unique queries with DO=1 (triggering signing) and 200 cached queries with DO=1 (cache hits), measures total elapsed time for each batch, computes the latency ratio, and prints a proof string if the ratio exceeds 3x confirming per-query cryptographic overhead.
limitations_or_blockers: The PoC was not executed during this session due to build time constraints. The reproduction bundle is self-contained and can be run independently via ./reproduction/run.sh.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable ref, and mounts that checkout into the container.
